item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Indestructible unknown metal alloy that makes spooky relections… Pretty cliche. Besides, the Stage 2 reflections try so hard to be uncanny and eerie that to me they seem more comical than scary. And that last log with its "if spooky reflection dies you die too" just makes it even more cliche.

-1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as no source found indicates it has been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reflections that move on their own are one of my worst irrational fears, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a fairly good point, actually. A revision to Safe might be in order.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. Although I don't see why it isn't Safe instead of Euclid. If I understood correctly, you could just put it into a cardboard box, seal it against light and keep it contained forever at a cost similar to zero. Am I missing something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely done, works cohesively and well. Particular fan of the pyromaniac D-class!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the one hand, i'm very upset that the closest thing i've had to a good SCP idea is way too similar to this to be any good now.

On the other, this is very good, and it is quite possible that i never would have written/attempted to post mine, so …

+1.

item.title: Thank you.
item.content:encoded: This is the type of thing I have night terrors about. I loved it. Although my night terrors actually killed me in my dream. UPVOTED! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really don't care for this concept.

But the last test log plus the impeccable writing pushed me into an upvote. So… well done, author. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm…….

And it'd explain why it visibly suppresses them during Phase 1 and 2 - if it can survive intact for a time without its host during Stage 3, it only needs them for mobility and could theoretically still spread itself, but if it's unable to do so before then, it would want them docile or unthreatened…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just wanted to say that I enjoyed the last test.

Also, does killing the host also kill the parasite? If so, is there a time delay, or does it die immediately? Because that'd be kind of creepy to have the corpse lying on the ground while its "reflection" was still moving around, even if it was only for a little bit.

But if it doesn't die with the host, then that could shine a new light on its "death threats" to the hosts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, revisions in progress now - patching some grammar and readability bits, and starting to work on the Big Reveal.

Now, I'm trying to make a decision towards the end - would it add or subtract to the creepy if being infected once and surviving made you apparently immune to re-infection from SCP-1620 or another person's Stage 3? It could mean you'd been 'vaccinated', so to speak, but it also might mean the creature wasn't actually dead, only reverted to a Stage 1-like hibernation.

EDIT: Okay, the REDACTED of Stage 2 is now open and visible, hopefully the language I used got the effect across properly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot. I believe that there were too many tests with its destructibility, and I also thought that the test with other animals was a bit meh. Unlike some others I didn't really mind the multitude of test logs. I thought that each one was testing a different thing, and each was a fresh idea. Minus the destructibility things. Have my 3am +1 if you would please!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice. Reminds me of the mirror whose reflections beg for their "life". +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, we do already have SCP-100-J, the steaming pile of shit that most newbie entries emerge from (and are subsequently purged and presumably thrown back into), so that might be a bit superfluous.
*is totally not an instance of 1903-J himself… whistle…*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to know what the parasites look like.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm thinking of removing the destructibility tests entirely. The part the Foundation cares about is the entities it generates, they wouldn't be trying to smash or destroy it aside from some sample testing. and Things What Are Indestructible is boring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I'd prefer this if it weren't indestructible. What if it was? "Oops, Johnson. We destroyed the SCP."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I'm not big on the reflections turning into sparkly unicorns, that very last line seals this. You may actually want to mention something about that sooner, because the whole time I was going, "Well, it's infectious, but what's the worst it can do? Scare you to death?" Not so.

I'll also mention that I really dig the latent pyromaniac in the first test.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder who will be the first to write up 1903-J, a miraculous phenomenon where newbs' first entries are somehow good. I'm a terrible procrastinator, so not me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
are you on the 914 experiment log? cause you very fine

trying that one tonight. it helps if you wheeze and avoid eye contact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So why's it need them docile in the first place, anyway?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm now wanting to come up with a good SCP-based chat-up line.

"Baby, that ass is Keter, 'cause yo skirt don't contain it."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But am I Safe, or Euclid?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That be some spookyfodder right there, sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait a minute… This was a first-time SCP entry, and a mirror-based one to boot… And it *worked*?
Sir if you will please come with me, we have a few questions to ask you. Don't mind the cuffs, or the guns, and please only respond to your new designation one-nine-oh-three…
(also, +1)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Something that exposes things they don't want to have to face? If it's an inhuman entity trying to mimic what they 'actually' look like as it sees it, it wouldn't be able to tell the difference between the things they want to believe about themselves and how they really see themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's cool, it was just the first thing I though of, and I think that the suggestion you have made is a good one. Just as long as it's not something that takes away from the creepiness of the concept. A reflection that behaves separately to its object is a lot scarier than a big greebly monster that makes a reflection behave separately to its object.

Edit: Ooh, how about the idea of residual self image - maybe it could start to look like how the person sees themself, and act in ways they feel they act?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That is an idea I can get behind.

+1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh…I don't really think that fits. The creature's life cycle is entirely contained within mirrors/reflective surfaces. If it turned into a shadow-creature at the end of its life cycle, that'd make it impossible or at least very difficult to spawn the next 'generation'.

Thoughts: Would it be out-of-tone for, rather than a simple [REDACTED], for SCP-XXXX-1's mature phase to be a reflection of the host's "true self", for lack of a better term, but distorted? An aggressive, violent person's SCP-XXXX-1 would be a hulking muscle-maniac, a greedy or predatory person's reflection turns lean and angular with a maw full of jagged teeth? Something that is still vaguely recognizable as you, but with your worst flaws exaggerated to a grotesque degree, could be even more disturbing than a completely inhuman monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep, I like this one, but I would consider removing the 'It turns into a horrifying [REDACTED]' bit. It's out of tone with the rest, but if you do, I would avoid describing anything really, what if it turns into a shadow-creature of some kind and then shows signs of moving to the person's shadow?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, still positive ratings, that's a good sign. Okay, issues to fix…

The [REDACTED] description: If it really is dragging down the article, I can try to put something in there, but I really don't know what. Lovecraftian tentacle monsters are stupid, and the only other thing I could come up with would be a 50's-sci-fi-Grey alien, which is both not-scary and too much emphasis on 'SCP-1620 is FROM SPAAAAAACE', which I don't want explicit at all. I'll try and brainstorm some suitably horrific visages and bounce them off people.

Test Logs: If they feel like padding, I can shunt them behind a collapsible tag or dump them to a sub-page. I'm loathe to just remove them entirely right now, though, because I felt they answered some - not all, but some - important questions about how 1620 and 1620-1 acted without clumsy exposition dumping. Really, the log I was most uncertain on was the last one, where the two creatures fight in the mirror and one of the hosts dies; it shows that separate instances of 1620-1 are individuals, and territorial/solitary 'hunters', and gives a bit of teeth to the need to keep 1620 contained, but it's also breaking the previously consistent rule of 'the mirror-entities can't actually affect the outside world', which could be good or bad.

Containment wording: Noted, that'll be easy to patch.

@Khym: Actually…I like that idea a lot. I'll need some time to figure out how/where to implement it, but I think it fits rather well with the progression of infection - at first it tries to pretend everything's normal to the host, then gives up and admits it's independent but still tries to be nice, and only in maturity does it drop all pretenses because it doesn't need them docile anymore.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The parasite openly insulting and threatening the host isn't something I find creepy; it makes the parasite seem juvenile (this would be okay during the infectious phase, since that sort of behavior might help it spread). For me, what would be creepy would be if it tried to be friendly, but couldn't quite get it right. And while the host's back was turned, it would stare at the host with hatred and contempt, unconsciously miming harming the host, and maybe twitching a little.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1620 is to be contained in a sealed room with walls and ceiling painted a flat, non-reflective grey color, with its inner surface pointed away from the door at all times.

This…is not good. Right now it seems the ceiling has to have its inner surface pointed away from the door at all times. There are some other places where the article could use editing. Upvoted because it definitely holds promise, but you might want to tighten it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh to the test logs, meh to the expungement. Remove both and you have an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, not bad at all, but I agree that the test logs just seem like so much padding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, I like it. Tenuously upvoted due to the redaction and the rather dull if not unnecessary testing logs.

item.title: Re: Author Commentary
item.content:encoded: Well I'm glad you posted this and not that.

item.title: Re: Author Commentary
item.content:encoded:
Much appreciated.

Copied from my Announcement, since I forgot it here.

Appreciation/Thanks Credits: [Jasper] found me the perfect image, [murphy_slaw] gave me the brainstorm for the primary SCP's form, a bunch of other people I can't remember well enough to name individually helped in the IRC channel for fine-tweaking it.

Also, you didn't see my first-first SCP, the one that died unborn on the Sandbox…basically a veiled ripoff of the Monkey's Paw, with five fingers and a penchant for collateral damage instead of malicious wish-twisting. Cliches 5,10,18, and a bit of 2 all rolled into one, with a bit of 21 from the 'origin story' of two bumbling agents discovering it in a junk shop. I think that was like a vaccination of awful to clear the way for this.

item.title: Re: Author Commentary
item.content:encoded:
Man, I didn't think I could stomach another reflection SCP, but this one is pretty darned good. Why can't every first SCP be as decent as this one?
+1.

item.title: Author Commentary
item.content:encoded:
My first SCP that I'm willing to admit to/that made it off my sandbox page.

Basic idea seed - your reflection is one of the most mundane things ever - utterly predictable, utterly controllable…so what if it wasn't? What if your reflection wasn't actually your reflection, but a something else pretending to be your reflection - and the longer it stayed with you, the worse and worse it got at keeping up pretenses?

The weakest point I can see, as pointed out to me in the IRC, is [Redacting] the visual description of SCP-1620-1 in its Stage 3. Hiding the horror might be a crutch for new/lazy writers, but I just can't think of anything really solid that fits with the article's mood while still being scary.

I'm aware of SCP-919, the closest existing article in theme to this, but I think I'm sufficiently differentiated that my 'thing what lives in mirrors' stands on its own.

…That's it, I guess. Tear it apart, people.
